item.title: Why the US is losing Chinese AI stars
item.description: More entrepreneurs from China believe there are now greater opportunities at home than in Silicon Valley

item.title: How long can China defy history and logic with its imbalances?
item.description: The country is demanding far too much of a world fixated on cheap consumer goods

item.title: Taiwan’s drone ambitions play to its tech manufacturing strengths
item.description: Scaling up brings economies of scale, and the island is taking another lesson from China’s playbook: dual-use technology

item.title: The real barrier to Africa’s solar energy boom
item.description: Reducing the cost of capital is the only way to ensure the expansion of renewable energy across the continent

item.title: India should listen to its frustrated youth
item.description: Gen Z protests reflect young people’s growing anxiety about their future

item.title: The battle over America’s War Powers Act
item.description: Trump, like Nixon before him, believes Congress should not constrain his belligerence

item.title: The Lucid dream can continue on a bed of Saudi money
item.description: With Riyadh’s backing, the electric-car maker could get through this crunch period

item.title: The deed thieves targeting New York brownstones
item.description: As property values rise, more titles are being stolen from homeowners without their knowledge

item.title: The West Country: the UK’s most dysfunctional property market
item.description: Unsold homes are piling up as locals can’t afford to buy properties at a price that homeowners are willing to sell for

item.title: Burnham can be the failure that Britain needs
item.description: Big government must be tested to destruction for serious reform to finally happen

item.title: Warsh’s balance sheet consultants
item.description: Plus, tariffs force trading down, not price cuts

item.title: Who will win the war of neo-mercantilists?
item.description: China’s high degree of self-sufficiency also reduces its vulnerability to coercion by others

item.title: Investors could do with their own ‘Number 10 North’
item.description: Those willing to venture outside London should find they have less competition for good companies

item.title: How shifts in capital flows could change the dollar’s role
item.description: The push and pull of capital is setting up a long-term battle for the US currency

item.title: The high price of insider information
item.description: Without tough enforcement, prediction platforms provide fertile ground for manipulation

item.title: How a simulator won the office sweepstake
item.description: A maximalist World Cup made it harder than ever to predict outcomes — so I built a model

item.title: Should I change my financial adviser?
item.description: I’m getting divorced and am worried our adviser is too close to my husband and won’t act in my best interests

item.title: Japan’s trains are getting much faster as the country embraces moonshots
item.description: 505km per hour rail project has taken on symbolic importance

item.title: Meta’s unwinding of Manus shows even forced deals have merits
item.description: Disposal of Chinese agentic AI start-up has left the Facebook owner better off than it might seem

item.title: The EU needs to hold together on Russia sanctions
item.description: Cost of trying to protect domestic interests may be to embolden Moscow

item.title: Should testosterone be in Hegseth’s build-your-own-warrior playbook?
item.description: The US administration sees scientific evidence as an optional, flexible handmaid to the more vital business of masculinity

item.title: The bear hug is an increasingly sensible way to play UK M&A
item.description: Negotiations are being played out in public — no bad thing given boards and investors may see things differently

item.title: Six foolish mistakes for the new chancellor to avoid
item.description: John Healey will be under pressure on many fronts, but bear-pits are lurking . . .

item.title: Why the world trusts China more than America
item.description: Countries suspicious of the US are to be found on every continent, not just in its own back yard

item.title: A break with the Starmer era — except at the Treasury
item.description: Chancellor from Labour’s ‘old right’ is experienced but unlikely to meaningfully diverge from previous set up
